what: what does lexical mean in javascript
what determines variable access in javascript
why: why can inner functions access outer variables
why does scope depend on where code is written
how: how does js decide variable access
how does lexical scope determine variable lookup
how does a function know where to look for a variable
compare: where function is defined vs where it is called
output: inner function can read outer variable
function remembers variables from definition site not call site

1_beginner_explanation.summary: Lexical Scope (also called Static Scope) means that a function's access to variables is determined by **where the function is written in the source code** — not where it is called from. JavaScript locks in the scope at **definition time**, not at call time. An inner function written inside an outer function will always have access to the outer function's variables, no matter where or how the inner function is eventually called.
1_beginner_explanation.analogy: Lexical scope is like citizenship by birthplace. Wherever you travel and work in the world (wherever the function is called), you carry the rights and access of the country you were born in (where the function was defined). Your access rights don't change based on which country you're visiting today.

2_internal_mechanism.execution_flow: When a function object is created, the engine attaches a hidden `[[Environment]]` slot to it containing a reference to the Lexical Environment that was active at the moment of creation. When the function is later called — possibly from a completely different place — the engine restores this captured outer environment as the outer reference for the new Lexical Environment. Variable lookups always walk this predetermined chain.
2_internal_mechanism.memory: The `[[Environment]]` reference is set once at function creation and never changes. This is what makes JavaScript 'lexically scoped' — the resolution path is baked in at parse/compile time.

3_real_world_example.use_case: Demonstrating that a function exported from a module always uses the variables from the module it was defined in — not variables from wherever the caller imports it.

4_common_confusion.trap: Thinking that if you call a function from a place where a variable exists, the function can access that variable. It cannot — scope is determined by definition site, never call site.
4_common_confusion.fix: Ask 'Where is this function *written*?' not 'Where is it *called* from?' — that determines what it can see.

6_edge_case.quirk: `this` does NOT follow lexical scope rules in regular functions — it is dynamic and depends on how the function is called. Arrow functions are the exception: they capture `this` lexically (from their definition site), which is why they are so useful as callbacks inside methods.
